title: Christmas Day
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
## Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
## December 25, 2021 | Preacher: Richard Lawson

LITURGICAL CONTEXT
Christmas Day falls on a Saturday in 2021, making this the principal celebration of the Nativity of Our Lord Jesus Christ in the liturgical calendar. The readings for Christmas Day morning typically follow the third set of propers: Isaiah 52:7-10, Psalm 98, Hebrews 1:1-12, and John 1:1-14 (the Prologue). The sermon occurs at the culmination of the Advent season and marks the beginning of the Christmas season proper, which extends through Candlemas (February 2).

EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS
The Episcopal Church was in the period following the 80th General Convention (held in 2018), with the 81st General Convention scheduled for summer 2022 in Austin, Texas. In the Mountain West Region, discussions around pandemic recovery and declining attendance were ongoing concerns for congregations. The Diocese of Colorado (which includes Saint John's Cathedral in Denver) was navigating post-vaccine return-to-in-person worship with varying comfort levels among parishioners regarding mask policies and gathering size.

DENVER & COLORADO NEWS
Denver experienced unusually mild December weather leading up to Christmas 2021, with temperatures in the 50s Fahrenheit on Christmas Eve and Christmas Day itself—atypical for the season and contributing to a green Christmas without snow. The Denver metro area was dealing with ongoing COVID-19 case increases (the Omicron variant was beginning to surge nationally and locally), creating uncertainty about holiday gatherings. City officials had lifted most pandemic restrictions by late 2021, though healthcare systems were preparing for a winter surge.

NATIONAL NEWS
The Omicron COVID-19 variant was rapidly becoming dominant, with case numbers surging across the United States in mid-to-late December, creating anxiety about holiday gatherings and hospital capacity. President Joe Biden faced criticism for his handling of pandemic messaging, having declared the pandemic "over" in September but confronting new waves of infections. The Kyle Rittenhouse acquittal (November 19) continued to dominate cultural and political conversation, with ongoing debates about vigilantism and protest rights.

INTERNATIONAL NEWS
The world was watching the Taliban's consolidation of power in Afghanistan following the August 2021 withdrawal, with reports of humanitarian crisis and persecution of ethnic minorities and women. The Omicron variant was spreading rapidly across Europe and globally, prompting new travel restrictions and lockdowns in some countries. China was maintaining strict zero-COVID policies while preparing for the 2022 Winter Olympics in Beijing (scheduled for February), creating international scrutiny.

CULTURAL MOMENT
The national mood was marked by pandemic fatigue and cautious optimism about vaccines, mixed with uncertainty about new variants and politicized divisions over public health measures. Americans were attempting to return to "normal" Christmas celebrations after two years of pandemic disruption, though with lingering anxiety. The cultural conversation was fractured along political lines regarding pandemic response, civil unrest, and the direction of the country, creating a sense of deep national division during what is traditionally a season of unity and peace.
